You can use the Touch Bar to display interactive content and controls for supported models of MacBook Pro.
The class shows the available use cases.
When you select one of the use cases, the secondary view shows what you can do with the Touch Bar controls.
For example, the class shows how to use an object.
Refer to this sample if you’re looking for specific ways to use and customize the .
It shows how to create an for use with buttons, views, pickers, sliders, popovers, scrubbers, text fields, and text views.
The sample also displays a secondary window that shows a typical use case with the Touch Bar that uses an to pick an image that changes the window background.
This sample shows how to use the Cocoa document architecture to manage documents.
Apps must use Xcode’s Document Types editor to define the types of documents they can open.

The Cocoa document architecture uses the Objective-C runtime, and document-based apps often use Objective-C technologies such as key-value coding (KVC), key-value observing (KVO), Cocoa bindings, and Cocoa archiving ( ).
Therefore, the model classes in your app should be Objective-C classes (subclasses of ), and the properties and methods in those classes should be Objective-C compatible (declared ).
In addition, their properties should be declared in Swift, which tells the compiler to use dynamic dispatch to access that attribute.
When you create your subclass, you determine which window controller the document will use by implementing the function.
The systems provide easy-to-use and highly capable APIs for text editing, such as , , and SwiftUI .
Apps generally use these APIs to implement text editing.
You use an object to manage the items that appear in a toolbar, which are objects created in Interface Builder or source code.
This sample shows how to use to navigate hierarchical data.
When you use with , your app presents data in a primary-detail format similar to that of the Xcode Project navigator or the Finder.
For a primary-detail configuration, use , which arranges two or more views in a linear stack running horizontally.
The class focuses on how to use .
In a primary-detail user interface, the list view on the left must remain the same size when the split view grows or shrinks, while the detail view on the right changes size.
Use the Edit menu to directly affect the contents of the outline view.
Use the protocol to enable or disable the Delete menu item according to the selection in the outline view.
Use the window restoration system’s function to save the outline view’s node selection.
The next time the app launches, use to restore that selection.
This sample shows how to use with each individual view either disclosed (expanded) or hidden (collapsed).
For state restoration to work, you must deselect the “Close windows when quitting an app” checkbox on the General Pane in System Preferences.
To reset state restoration, use Command-Option-Quit when quitting the sample.
To restore the stack view’s window on relaunch, use the protocol.
This sample provides two disclosure options: A triangle A rounded button The sample project conditionally decides which type of header disclosure control to use by referencing the compilation flag.
If you prefer the style (a rounded button with up and down arrows), remove the compilation flag from the Xcode target’s build settings.
The person should be able to return to where they left off—and window state restoration is a core part of making that experience seamless.
This sample app demonstrates the use of state preservation and restoration for a macOS app.
To enable window state restoration, each window must be configured with a unique identifier, a frame autosave name, and a restoration class.
The app delegate must also declare support for secure restorable state:
Whenever someone makes a change that should be preserved—selecting a product, switching tabs, or editing fields—the window controller calls .
Do not force quit the app, as macOS discards saved state when a person force quits.
When working with drag and drop, use file promises to indicate intent for a future drag operation.
Use the class when creating file promises.
Use the to receive file promises.
This sample app also supports drag and drop of images from within the collection view that don’t involve a URL.

The sample registers these pasteboard types in the view controller’s function: Handle file promises before handling file URLs, because the file promise generally represents the higher-quality image and should take precedence when both types are supported.

The framework also contains built-in support for localization and accessibility to ensure that your app reaches as many people as possible.
